passages.explanation: During those days another large crowd gathers. Since they have nothing to eat, Jesus calls his disciples: I have compassion for these people — they've been with me three days and have nothing to eat. If I send them home hungry, they'll collapse on the way, because some have come a long distance. His disciples answer: where in this remote place can anyone get enough bread to feed them? He asks: how many loaves do you have? Seven, they say. He tells the crowd to sit down. He takes the seven loaves, gives thanks, breaks them, and gives them to his disciples to distribute. They also have a few small fish. He gives thanks for those and tells the disciples to distribute them. The people eat and are satisfied. The disciples pick up seven baskets of broken pieces. About four thousand are present. He sends them away and gets into a boat with his disciples to the region of Dalmanutha. The second feeding happens in Gentile territory (the Decapolis). The first feeding yielded twelve baskets (the twelve tribes of Israel). This one yields seven (the number of completeness, often associated with Gentile nations). Jesus feeds Israel and the nations. The disciples' response — 'where can anyone get enough bread?' — is bewildering given they saw the first miracle. They're not learning.

The Pharisees come and begin to question Jesus. To test him, they ask for a sign from heaven. He sighs deeply and says: why does this generation ask for a sign? Truly I tell you, no sign will be given to it. Then he leaves them, gets back into the boat, and crosses to the other side. Jesus has just fed four thousand people with seven loaves. The Pharisees want a sign. The deep sigh expresses something beyond frustration — it's grief at wilful blindness. They've already seen more signs than they can process.

The disciples have forgotten to bring bread, except for one loaf they have in the boat. Jesus warns them: be careful — watch out for the yeast of the Pharisees and that of Herod. They discuss this with each other: it's because we have no bread. Jesus knows what they're saying: why are you talking about having no bread? Do you still not see or understand? Are your hearts hardened? Do you have eyes but fail to see, and ears but fail to hear? When I broke the five loaves for the five thousand, how many baskets of pieces did you pick up? Twelve, they answer. And when I broke the seven loaves for the four thousand, how many baskets did you pick up? Seven. He asks: do you still not understand? Jesus fires off a rapid series of questions — eight in a row. The disciples are worried about physical bread while sitting in a boat with the man who multiplied loaves twice. The yeast of the Pharisees is demanding signs despite evidence. The yeast of Herod is political calculation. Both blind you. And the disciples are showing symptoms of both.

They come to Bethsaida, and people bring a blind man and beg Jesus to touch him. Jesus takes the blind man by the hand, leads him outside the village, spits on his eyes, and places his hands on him. He asks: do you see anything? The man looks up: I see people — they look like trees walking around. Once more Jesus puts his hands on the man's eyes. Then his eyes are opened, his sight is restored, and he sees everything clearly. Jesus sends him home: don't even go into the village. This is the only two-stage miracle in the Gospels. It's placed here deliberately — right between Jesus' exasperation at the disciples' blindness and Peter's partial confession. The man sees, but blurrily at first. Then clearly. That's exactly the disciples' trajectory: they see something, but not clearly. Full sight is coming, but it takes a second touch.

Jesus and his disciples go to the villages around Caesarea Philippi. On the way he asks: who do people say I am? They reply: some say John the Baptist, others say Elijah, and still others one of the prophets. He asks: but what about you — who do you say I am? Peter answers: you are the Messiah. Jesus warns them not to tell anyone about him. Peter's answer is correct — but as the next passage shows, his understanding of what 'Messiah' means is wrong. That's why Jesus tells them to keep quiet. A premature announcement of a misunderstood messiahship would be worse than silence.

Jesus begins to teach them that the Son of Man must suffer many things, be rejected by the elders, the chief priests, and the teachers of the law, and be killed, and after three days rise again. He speaks plainly about this. Peter takes him aside and begins to rebuke him. But when Jesus turns and looks at his disciples, he rebukes Peter: get behind me, Satan! You do not have in mind the concerns of God, but merely human concerns. This is the sharpest rebuke Jesus gives anyone in the Gospels — and it's directed at Peter, minutes after his great confession. Peter's idea of the Messiah has no room for suffering and death. Jesus identifies that resistance as satanic, because it mirrors the wilderness temptation: glory without the cross. 'Get behind me' means 'get back in the disciple's position' — following, not leading.

Jesus calls the crowd to him along with his disciples. He says: whoever wants to be my disciple must deny themselves, take up their cross, and follow me. For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for me and for the gospel will save it. What good is it for someone to gain the whole world, yet forfeit their soul? Or what can anyone give in exchange for their soul? If anyone is ashamed of me and my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, the Son of Man will be ashamed of them when he comes in his Father's glory with the holy angels. Jesus doesn't just tell the disciples about his cross — he tells the crowd to carry their own. The cross isn't a metaphor for difficulty; it's a Roman execution device. Jesus is saying: the path I'm walking — toward rejection, suffering, and death — is the same path you must choose. And it's the only path that leads to life.
